Ablation name: Remove Error Classification System.
I'll implement the ablation study that removes the error classification system. The key changes are: (1) replace the fine-grained `classify_error` function with a simple version that always returns 'generic', (2) modify the failure library to use a flat structure instead of categorizing by error type, (3) change the retrieval function to not filter by error category, and (4) update all related logic to work with this simplified approach. This will test whether the sophisticated error categorization provides actual value or if the multi-stage refinement benefits come primarily from iterative refinement itself.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")

try:
    experiment_data = np.load(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), allow_pickle=True
    ).item()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error loading experiment data: {e}")
    experiment_data = {}

datasets = ["synthetic_code_generation", "code_contests", "mbpp"]

# Plot 1: Training and Validation Loss Curves for All Datasets
try:
    plt.figure(figsize=(14, 5))
    for idx, dataset_name in enumerate(datasets):
        plt.subplot(1, 3, idx + 1)
        data = experiment_data["multi_stage_refinement"][dataset_name]
        train_loss = data["losses"]["train"]
        val_loss = data["losses"]["val"]
        epochs = range(len(train_loss))
        plt.plot(
            epochs, train_loss, "o-", label="Train Loss", linewidth=2, markersize=6
        )
        plt.plot(epochs, val_loss, "s-", label="Val Loss", linewidth=2, markersize=6)
        plt.xlabel("Configuration Epoch", fontsize=10)
        plt.ylabel("Loss (1 - Pass@1)", fontsize=10)
        plt.title(
            f'{dataset_name.replace("_", " ").title()}\nAblation: No Error Classification',
            fontsize=11,
            fontweight="bold",
        )
        plt.legend()
        plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_training_validation_loss_all_datasets.png"),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating training/validation loss plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 2: Pass@1 Accuracy Comparison Across Datasets
try:
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    for dataset_name in datasets:
        data = experiment_data["multi_stage_refinement"][dataset_name]
        metrics = data["metrics"]["val"]
        epochs = range(len(metrics))
        plt.plot(
            epochs,
            metrics,
            "o-",
            label=dataset_name.replace("_", " ").title(),
            linewidth=2,
            markersize=8,
        )
    plt.xlabel("Configuration Epoch", fontsize=12)
    plt.ylabel("Pass@1 Accuracy", fontsize=12)
    plt.title(
        "Ablation Study: Pass@1 Performance Without Error Classification\nAcross All Datasets",
        fontsize=13,
        fontweight="bold",
    )
    plt.legend(fontsize=10)
    plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_pass_at_1_comparison_all_datasets.png"),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating Pass@1 comparison plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 3: Error Reduction Rates by Configuration
try:
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    x_offset = np.array([-0.2, 0, 0.2])
    for idx, dataset_name in enumerate(datasets):
        data = experiment_data["multi_stage_refinement"][dataset_name]
        error_reductions = data["error_type_tracking"]
        configs = range(len(error_reductions))
        plt.bar(
            np.array(configs) + x_offset[idx],
            error_reductions,
            width=0.18,
            label=dataset_name.replace("_", " ").title(),
            alpha=0.8,
        )
    plt.xlabel("Configuration (C1-C4)", fontsize=12)
    plt.ylabel("Error Reduction Rate (%)", fontsize=12)
    plt.title(
        "Ablation: Semantic Error Reduction Without Error Classification\nComparison Across Datasets",
        fontsize=13,
        fontweight="bold",
    )
    plt.axhline(y=0, color="black", linestyle="-", linewidth=0.5)
    plt.legend(fontsize=10)
    plt.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)
    plt.xticks(
        range(4),
        ["C1\n(1 stage)", "C2\n(2 stages)", "C3\n(3 stages)", "C4\n(3 stages)"],
    )
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(
            working_dir, "ablation_error_reduction_by_config_all_datasets.png"
        ),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating error reduction plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 4: Stage Analysis - Average Stages Used
try:
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    for dataset_name in datasets:
        data = experiment_data["multi_stage_refinement"][dataset_name]
        predictions = data["predictions"]
        avg_stages = [
            predictions[f"config_{i}"]["analysis"]["avg_stages"]
            for i in range(len(predictions))
        ]
        plt.plot(
            range(len(avg_stages)),
            avg_stages,
            "o-",
            label=dataset_name.replace("_", " ").title(),
            linewidth=2,
            markersize=8,
        )
    plt.xlabel("Configuration Epoch", fontsize=12)
    plt.ylabel("Average Refinement Stages", fontsize=12)
    plt.title(
        "Ablation: Stage Utilization Without Error Classification\nAverage Number of Refinement Stages Used",
        fontsize=13,
        fontweight="bold",
    )
    plt.legend(fontsize=10)
    plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "ablation_stage_utilization_all_datasets.png"),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating stage utilization plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 5: Improvement Over Baseline
try:
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
    for dataset_name in datasets:
        data = experiment_data["multi_stage_refinement"][dataset_name]
        predictions = data["predictions"]
        improvements = [
            predictions[f"config_{i}"]["improvement"] for i in range(len(predictions))
        ]
        improvements_pct = [imp * 100 for imp in improvements]
        plt.plot(
            range(len(improvements_pct)),
            improvements_pct,
            "o-",
            label=dataset_name.replace("_", " ").title(),
            linewidth=2,
            markersize=8,
        )
    plt.axhline(
        y=0, color="red", linestyle="--", linewidth=1.5, alpha=0.7, label="Baseline"
    )
    plt.xlabel("Configuration Epoch", fontsize=12)
    plt.ylabel("Improvement Over Baseline (%)", fontsize=12)
    plt.title(
        "Ablation: Performance Improvement Without Error Classification\nRelative to Baseline Across Configurations",
        fontsize=13,
        fontweight="bold",
    )
    plt.legend(fontsize=10)
    plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(
            working_dir, "ablation_improvement_over_baseline_all_datasets.png"
        ),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating improvement plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

Ablation name: Remove Problem Complexity-Based Stage Adaptation.
I'll implement the ablation study that removes the problem complexity-based stage adaptation mechanism. The key change is in the `multi_stage_refinement` function where instead of computing `num_stages = max(1, min(max_stages, int(complexity * max_stages) + 1))` based on problem complexity, all problems will use `num_stages = max_stages` uniformly. This tests whether adaptive resource allocation based on problem difficulty provides benefits over a uniform approach. The complexity estimation function will be kept for tracking purposes but won't influence stage allocation.

plot_plan: I'll create visualizations for the multi-stage refinement experiment without complexity adaptation. The experiment evaluates code generation across three datasets (synthetic, code_contests, and mbpp) using different refinement configurations. I'll plot training/validation metrics, pass@1 rates across configurations, error reduction rates, correction rates by error category, and stage utilization patterns.

plot_analyses.analysis: Across synthetic_code_generation, increasing configuration complexity (C1→C4) monotonically reduces both train/val loss (loss drops from ~0.05 at C1 to ~0 at C3/C4) and increases pass@1 (≈0.95→≈1.0). Relative improvement over baseline is ~0% at C1 and ~4% (C2) to ~5.2–5.3% (C3/C4), suggesting most gains come from enabling >1 refinement stage, with diminishing returns beyond 3 stages. Stage utilization rises from 1.0 (C1) to ~1.25 (C2–C4), indicating the system rarely needs the full budget even when available. Final error correction rate for the shown category (algorithmic) is 1.0, consistent with near-perfect pass@1 and near-zero loss; however, this may indicate the synthetic set is too easy or has limited error diversity, so it is less diagnostic for component ablations beyond confirming no regressions.

On MBPP (no complexity adaptation), pass@1 is flat at ~0.90 for C1–C4, above the baseline (~0.885), implying multi-stage refinement helps but additional stages do not. Semantic error reduction is also flat (~13%) across configurations, reinforcing that extra refinement budget isn’t being converted into additional correctness. Stage utilization increases steadily (1.0→1.3), meaning the system is spending more refinement steps without measurable gain—an efficiency red flag and a sign that feedback/adaptive selection (currently ablated/disabled per plot title) may be required to stop early or target fixes. Category-level correction (algorithmic) varies by configuration (C1=1.0, C2=0.5, C3≈0.67, C4=1.0), suggesting high variance/small sample sizes for category breakdown; aggregate metrics being constant despite swings implies the category plot may be based on few instances or a different slice than pass@1.

The cross-dataset scatter (max refinement stages vs pass@1) shows synthetic_code_generation benefits strongly from more stages (≈0.95 at 1 stage, ≈0.99 at 2, ≈1.0 at 3), MBPP is invariant (~0.90 regardless of stage budget), and code_contests is nearly invariant (~0.37–0.375). This indicates dataset sensitivity: refinement budget alone is not the bottleneck for harder benchmarks; the limiting factor is likely error detection quality, feedback signal usefulness, or retrieval relevance. It also suggests that for MBPP and code_contests, adaptive stage selection should prioritize early stopping and targeted edits rather than simply allowing more stages.

The multi-dataset line plot (pass@1 across configurations, no complexity adaptation) confirms the same pattern: synthetic improves with configuration index (≈0.95→1.0), while MBPP stays at 0.90 and code_contests stays ~0.37–0.375. The plateau on code_contests despite more stages suggests either (i) the model fails to localize/repair semantic issues from feedback, (ii) tests are too sparse/ambiguous to guide refinement, or (iii) the refinement prompts are not contest-specific (e.g., missing constraints like time complexity). This plot is useful for the ablation stage: if removing feedback/error detection doesn’t change these flat curves, it may indicate those components are not actually active/effective under the “no adaptation” setting.

On code_contests (no complexity adaptation), pass@1 increases slightly from ~0.369 (C1) to ~0.375 (C2–C4), above baseline (~0.344), but then saturates. Semantic error reduction rises from ~3.8% (C1) to ~4.7–4.8% (C2–C4), again saturating. Stage utilization increases sharply (1.0→~1.75→~2.38 then slightly down to ~2.25), meaning additional refinement is being used but yields minimal gains—suggesting diminishing returns and possibly compounding errors in later stages. Category correction (logic) improves with configuration (C1≈0.5, C2≈0.88, C3=1.0, C4=1.0), which is stronger than the aggregate pass@1 change; this mismatch again hints at small category sample or that ‘logic’ errors are only a subset of failures and other error types dominate overall pass@1.

The grouped bar chart of semantic error reduction by dataset across configurations shows synthetic_code_generation improves strongly with configuration (≈82%→≈95%→100%→100%), while MBPP stays ~13% and code_contests stays ~4–5%. This is a clear dataset-specific sensitivity signal: the current refinement/feedback mechanism is highly effective when errors are ‘simple/structured’ (synthetic) but not when tasks require deeper algorithmic reasoning (MBPP) or complex constraints (code_contests). For the planned component ablations, this plot provides a baseline expectation: removing feedback/error detection should most visibly hurt synthetic, but to demonstrate value on real benchmarks you likely need improved retrieval/feedback specificity to move MBPP/code_contests beyond their plateaus.

The synthetic_code_generation panel (explicitly labeled no complexity adaptation) reiterates: pass@1 climbs from ~0.95 (C1) to ~0.99 (C2) to 1.0 (C3/C4); semantic error reduction climbs ~82%→~95%→100%→100%. Stage utilization rises to ~1.25 at C2 and then stays flat, suggesting that even without adaptation, the system converges quickly and doesn’t need extra stages. Category correction shows perfect correction for C3/C4 but missing/zero bars for C1/C2 in the displayed category, which may indicate either no algorithmic errors occurred in those configs or the plotting logic only logs corrected errors when present—worth clarifying because it affects interpretability of category ablations.

The code_contests training/validation loss plot shows val loss drops from ~0.631 at C1 to ~0.625 at C2 and then stays flat through C4; relative improvement over baseline is ~0% at C1 and ~1.7% at C2–C4. Stage utilization matches earlier (1.0→1.75→2.38→2.25). Final error correction rate for ‘logic’ is 1.0, which conflicts with the modest overall pass@1 (~0.375) and small semantic error reduction (~4–5%); this likely means the ‘final error correction rate’ metric is computed on a narrow subset (only instances where a particular error category was detected) rather than on all failures. This should be explicitly defined in the paper to avoid overstating correction ability.

The MBPP training/validation loss plot is completely flat at ~0.10 across configurations, and the relative improvement panel is empty/zeroed. Yet other MBPP plots show pass@1 improvement over baseline and ~13% semantic error reduction. This inconsistency suggests a logging/plotting bug: either loss is not being updated per configuration, or the loss metric is computed differently than pass@1 (e.g., using a fixed baseline or different split). Before drawing ablation conclusions, fix this instrumentation; otherwise component contribution estimates based on loss deltas will be unreliable. Stage utilization increases (1.0→1.1→1.2→1.3) and final error correction rate (algorithmic) is 1.0, again suggesting the correction-rate metric is conditional and not directly comparable to pass@1.

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots cover three datasets under a ‘no complexity adaptation’ setting across configurations C1–C4 (increasing max refinement stages). Synthetic_code_generation shows strong gains with more stages (pass@1 to 1.0; semantic error reduction to 100%) with modest stage usage (~1.25). MBPP and code_contests show small or flat gains despite increased stage utilization, indicating refinement budget alone is insufficient; feedback/error detection/retrieval quality likely bottlenecks. Several metric inconsistencies appear (MBPP loss flat and relative improvement empty; ‘final error correction rate’ at 1.0 despite low pass@1), suggesting definitions or instrumentation issues that must be clarified before component contribution claims.

Ablation name: Remove Category-Specific Failure Pattern Retrieval.
I will implement an ablation study that removes the category-specific failure pattern retrieval system. Instead of using `retrieve_failure_patterns_by_category` which matches patterns based on classified error categories, the ablated version will use simple random retrieval that ignores error classifications. This tests whether the sophisticated error categorization provides value over generic pattern matching. The experiment will compare the baseline multi-stage refinement (with category-specific retrieval) against the ablated version (with random retrieval) across all three datasets, tracking pass rates, error reduction, and correction effectiveness.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")

try:
    experiment_data = np.load(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), allow_pickle=True
    ).item()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error loading experiment data: {e}")
    experiment_data = {}

datasets = ["synthetic_code_generation", "code_contests", "mbpp"]
approaches = ["baseline_category_specific", "ablation_random_retrieval"]

# Plot 1: Training/Validation curves for all datasets
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(15, 10))
    for col, dataset in enumerate(datasets):
        for row, metric_type in enumerate(["metrics", "losses"]):
            ax = axes[row, col]
            for approach in approaches:
                if dataset in experiment_data.get(approach, {}):
                    data = experiment_data[approach][dataset][metric_type]["val"]
                    label = (
                        "Category-Specific"
                        if "baseline" in approach
                        else "Random Retrieval"
                    )
                    color = "blue" if "baseline" in approach else "red"
                    ax.plot(
                        data,
                        marker="o",
                        label=label,
                        linewidth=2,
                        color=color,
                        alpha=0.7,
                    )
            ylabel = "Pass@1 Rate" if metric_type == "metrics" else "Loss (1-Pass@1)"
            ax.set_xlabel("Configuration", fontsize=10)
            ax.set_ylabel(ylabel, fontsize=10)
            title = f'{dataset.replace("_", " ").title()}: {ylabel}'
            ax.set_title(title, fontsize=11, fontweight="bold")
            ax.legend(fontsize=9)
            ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "training_validation_curves_all_datasets.png"),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating training/validation curves: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 2: Performance comparison across datasets

plot_plan: This experiment compares category-specific failure pattern retrieval against random retrieval across three datasets (synthetic, code_contests, and mbpp) with multiple configurations. I'll create visualizations showing the training/validation curves, performance comparisons between baseline and ablation approaches, error correction rates, and stage utilization patterns. The plots will highlight the impact of removing category-specific retrieval on code refinement success rates.

plot_analyses.analysis:
Stage utilization across datasets (Category-Specific vs Random Retrieval, configs C1–C3):
- Synthetic Code Generation: average refinement stages are flat at ~1.0 for all configs for both retrieval strategies. This indicates the refinement loop is effectively not engaging beyond a single step on this dataset (either tasks are easy, stopping criteria trigger immediately, or the pipeline is configured to do only one stage).
- Code Contests: stage utilization differs by configuration: ~1.0 at C1, then jumps to ~1.62 at C2 and remains ~1.62 at C3. The two strategies overlap, implying retrieval type does not affect how many refinement stages are used; rather, configuration changes (C2/C3) drive additional refinement. This suggests the “adaptive stage selection / refinement budget” is activated primarily for harder contest tasks.
- MBPP: flat at ~1.0 across C1–C3 for both strategies, again suggesting minimal multi-stage refinement usage.
Implication for ablation goals: if you are trying to measure “changes in average refinement stages utilized” when ablating feedback/error detection/adaptive selection, these plots show that only Code Contests has headroom to observe stage-utilization effects. Synthetic/MBPP will likely show near-zero deltas unless you adjust stopping criteria or allow more stages.

Code Contests ablation panel (Baseline vs Ablation; Impact; Correction rates; Stage utilization):
- Pass@1 bars (Category-Specific vs Random Retrieval) are extremely close across C1–C3 (~0.37–0.375). The only visible gap is at C1 where Random Retrieval is slightly lower than Category-Specific.
- “Impact of Removing Category-Specific Retrieval” shows a small performance drop at C1 (~1.67%), and ~0% at C2 and C3. This indicates category-specific retrieval provides marginal benefit only in the C1 setting; once configuration changes in C2/C3 are enabled, the benefit disappears.
- Correction rates by category: only “logic” is shown and both strategies are at 1.0. This ceiling effect makes it impossible to attribute semantic-error improvements to retrieval strategy on this dataset from this plot; you need more granular categories or non-saturated metrics (e.g., fraction of failing tests fixed, or error-type distribution).
- Stage utilization repeats the earlier finding: ~1.0 at C1 and ~1.62 at C2/C3 with overlapping curves. So the extra refinement in C2/C3 does not translate into a measurable Pass@1 gain here (still ~0.375), suggesting either (a) refinements are not effective on contest problems, (b) evaluation is too coarse/small to see gains, or (c) the refinement is improving other metrics not shown (e.g., fewer runtime errors but still failing hidden tests).
Implication: For Code Contests, the dominant factor seems to be configuration (C2/C3 enabling more stages), but it increases compute without improving Pass@1. This is a red flag for the “feedback mechanism” value proposition unless you can show semantic-error reduction or partial credit improvements.

Pass@1 and loss (1−Pass@1) across datasets, Category-Specific vs Random Retrieval, configs encoded as 0/1/2:
- Synthetic Code Generation:
  - Category-Specific: Pass@1 rises from ~0.90 (config 0) to ~0.975 (configs 1 and 2). Corresponding loss drops from ~0.10 to ~0.025.
  - Random Retrieval: Pass@1 is ~0.962 (config 0), dips to ~0.937 (config 1), then rises to ~0.975 (config 2). Loss mirrors this (~0.038 → ~0.063 → ~0.025).
  - Interpretation: Category-specific retrieval underperforms random at config 0 but strongly outperforms at config 1; both converge at config 2. This suggests interactions between retrieval strategy and other components toggled by configuration (e.g., feedback/refinement settings). The non-monotonic random curve indicates config 1 may be destabilizing unless retrieval is well-matched.
- Code Contests:
  - Category-Specific is flat at ~0.375 across configs.
  - Random Retrieval improves from ~0.369 to ~0.375 by config 1 and stays there.
  - Loss changes are tiny (Category-Specific ~0.625 flat; Random ~0.631 → ~0.625). This suggests effects are within noise; you likely need confidence intervals / more seeds.
- MBPP:
  - Category-Specific: ~0.895 (config 0) → ~0.890 (config 1) → ~0.895 (config 2).
  - Random Retrieval: ~0.895 → ~0.900 → ~0.895.
  - Loss mirrors: Category-Specific peaks at ~0.11 at config 1; Random bottoms at ~0.10 at config 1.
  - Interpretation: At config 1, random retrieval slightly outperforms category-specific; otherwise identical. Again indicates either retrieval matching is not helping on MBPP, or the metric is saturated/insensitive.
Implication for component contribution quantification: the largest observable deltas are on Synthetic Code Generation, especially at config 1. Code Contests and MBPP show near-zero deltas, so any component ranking will be dominated by Synthetic unless you use more sensitive metrics (semantic error reduction, error-type breakdown, or pass@k).

Baseline vs Abla­tion performance comparison (Pass@1 bars for Category-Specific vs Random Retrieval across datasets and C1–C3):
- Synthetic Code Generation:
  - C1: Category-Specific ~0.90 vs Random ~0.96 (random better).
  - C2: Category-Specific ~0.98 vs Random ~0.94 (category-specific better).
  - C3: both ~0.98 (tie).
  - This reinforces a strong interaction between configuration and retrieval strategy; “removing category-specific retrieval” is not uniformly harmful.
- Code Contests:
  - C1: Category-Specific slightly higher than Random.
  - C2/C3: essentially identical.
- MBPP:
  - C1: identical.
  - C2: Random slightly higher.
  - C3: identical.
Implication: If the intended ablation is “remove category-specific retrieval,” the effect is configuration-dependent and sometimes beneficial to remove it (Synthetic C1, MBPP C2). This suggests either (a) category labels/retrieval queries are noisy/mismatched, (b) category-specific negatives overfit or distract for some settings, or (c) prompt length/format differs by config and interacts with retrieval content.

MBPP error correction rates (Category-Specific vs Random Retrieval):
- Only “algorithmic” category is shown, and both strategies have correction rate = 1.0.
- This is fully saturated and provides no discriminative evidence about which retrieval strategy improves semantic error prevention.
Implication: You need either more categories (logic, boundary, API, etc.), a non-ceiling metric, or report counts/denominators. Otherwise this plot cannot support claims about semantic error reduction on MBPP.

Code Contests error correction rates:
- Category-Specific: only “logic” shown, correction rate = 1.0.
- Random Retrieval: shows “logic” and “algorithmic,” both at 1.0.
- Again saturated at 1.0, and categories differ between strategies, making direct comparison hard (it’s unclear if categories are missing due to zero samples or plotting choice).
Implication: For a rigorous ablation, ensure the same set of categories with counts per category, and avoid ceiling effects by using more challenging evaluation or measuring partial correction (e.g., % failing tests fixed per refinement).

Impact of removing category-specific retrieval (performance drop % across datasets/configs):
- Synthetic Code Generation: C1 shows a negative drop (~−7%), meaning removing category-specific retrieval improves performance substantially in that setting; C2 shows a positive drop (~+3.9%), meaning removal hurts; C3 ~0.
- Code Contests: C1 shows a small positive drop (~+1.67%), C2/C3 ~0.
- MBPP: C2 shows a negative drop (~−1.1%), C1/C3 ~0.
Implication: The retrieval component is not consistently beneficial; it is highly configuration- and dataset-dependent. This is exactly the kind of interaction effect you should highlight: category-specific retrieval helps when the rest of the system is configured to leverage it (Synthetic C2), but can hurt when the system is in a simpler/earlier configuration (Synthetic C1) or when tasks are short/simple (MBPP). This also suggests adding a retrieval-confidence gate (only inject negatives if similarity above threshold) could prevent regressions.

Synthetic Code Generation error correction rates:
- Category-Specific: only “logic” shown, correction rate = 1.0.
- Random Retrieval: “boundary,” “algorithmic,” and “logic” shown, all at 1.0.
- All values are saturated at 1.0, and the category sets differ, preventing meaningful comparison.
Implication: These correction-rate plots, as currently constructed, cannot substantiate the paper’s central claim of reducing semantic errors by 23–31%. You likely need to report (i) pre-correction error incidence by category, (ii) post-guidance residual error rates, and (iii) absolute counts with confidence intervals.

MBPP summary panel (Baseline vs Ablation; Impact; Correction; Stage utilization):
- Pass@1: nearly identical across C1–C3 between Category-Specific and Random Retrieval (~0.89–0.90). At C2, Random is slightly higher.
- Impact plot shows a negative drop at C2 (~−1.1%), consistent with Random outperforming Category-Specific there; C1 and C3 are ~0.
- Correction rates by category: only algorithmic shown, both at 1.0 (ceiling).
- Stage utilization: flat at ~1.0 across configs and strategies.
Implication: On MBPP, category-specific retrieval does not provide measurable gains; the system is effectively single-stage and already strong. To demonstrate component contributions here, you may need to (a) increase difficulty (harder MBPP subset), (b) evaluate semantic error types beyond pass/fail, or (c) measure robustness (adversarial edge cases) where negative examples might matter.

Synthetic Code Generation summary panel (Baseline vs Ablation; Impact; Correction; Stage utilization):
- Pass@1: C1 Random (~0.96) > Category-Specific (~0.90); C2 Category-Specific (~0.98) > Random (~0.94); C3 tie (~0.98).
- Impact plot: C1 shows a large negative drop (~−7%) indicating category-specific retrieval is harmful there; C2 shows a positive drop (~+3.9%) indicating it is helpful; C3 ~0.
- Correction rates: only logic shown for both, at 1.0 (ceiling).
- Stage utilization: flat at ~1.0 across configs.
Implication: The biggest story is the sign flip between C1 and C2. This strongly suggests configuration C2 changes how retrieved failure patterns are integrated (format, number of examples, or gating), making category-specific retrieval beneficial only when integrated properly. This is valuable for the ablation section: retrieval quality alone isn’t enough; the feedback/prompting mechanism must be aligned to exploit it. However, because stage utilization is constant, the improvement is coming from better first-pass generation rather than more refinement.

vlm_feedback_summary: Across all provided figures, the comparisons are primarily between Category-Specific retrieval and Random retrieval across configurations C1–C3, reporting Pass@1, (1−Pass@1) loss, stage utilization, and error-correction rates by category. The main measurable effects appear on Synthetic Code Generation, where category-specific retrieval can either hurt (C1) or help (C2) and converges (C3), indicating strong interaction with configuration. Code Contests and MBPP show near-identical Pass@1 across strategies and mostly flat stage utilization (~1 stage), with Code Contests showing increased stages in C2/C3 without Pass@1 gains. Error-correction rate plots are saturated at 1.0 and often use inconsistent category sets, limiting their evidentiary value for semantic-error reduction claims.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")

try:
    experiment_data = np.load(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), allow_pickle=True
    ).item()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error loading experiment data: {e}")

datasets = ["synthetic_code_generation", "code_contests", "mbpp"]

# Plot 1: Training/Validation Curves for all datasets
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 4))
    for idx, dataset in enumerate(datasets):
        data = experiment_data["single_run_evaluation"][dataset]
        epochs = range(len(data["metrics"]["train"]))
        axes[idx].plot(
            epochs,
            data["metrics"]["train"],
            marker="o",
            label="Train Pass@1",
            linewidth=2,
        )
        axes[idx].plot(
            epochs, data["metrics"]["val"], marker="s", label="Val Pass@1", linewidth=2
        )
        axes[idx].set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
        axes[idx].set_ylabel("Pass@1 Rate")
        axes[idx].set_title(f'{dataset.replace("_", " ").title()}')
        axes[idx].legend()
        axes[idx].grid(alpha=0.3)
    plt.suptitle(
        "Training/Validation Pass@1 Curves Across Datasets (Single Run)",
        fontsize=14,
        fontweight="bold",
    )
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "all_datasets_training_curves.png"),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating training curves plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 2: Error Reduction Rates Comparison
try:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
    width = 0.25
    x = np.arange(4)
    for idx, dataset in enumerate(datasets):
        error_reductions = experiment_data["single_run_evaluation"][dataset][
            "error_type_tracking"
        ]
        ax.bar(
            x + idx * width,
            error_reductions,
            width,
            label=dataset.replace("_", " ").title(),
            alpha=0.8,
        )
    ax.set_xlabel("Configuration Index", fontsize=12)
    ax.set_ylabel("Semantic Error Reduction Rate (%)", fontsize=12)
    ax.set_title(
        "Semantic Error Reduction Rates Across Datasets (Single Run)",
        fontsize=14,
        fontweight="bold",
    )
    ax.set_xticks(x + width)
    ax.set_xticklabels(["C1", "C2", "C3", "C4"])
    ax.legend()
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)
    ax.axhline(y=0, color="black", linestyle="-", linewidth=0.8)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "all_datasets_error_reduction_comparison.png"),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating error reduction plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 3: Per-dataset detailed analysis (only for 2 representative datasets)
for dataset in ["synthetic_code_generation", "mbpp"]:
    try:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 10))
        data = experiment_data["single_run_evaluation"][dataset]

        # Subplot 1: Pass@1 progression
        predictions = data["predictions"]
        configs = sorted([k for k in predictions.keys() if k.startswith("config_")])
        scores = [predictions[c]["score"] for c in configs]
        improvements = [predictions[c]["improvement"] for c in configs]

        axes[0, 0].plot(
            range(len(scores)),
            scores,
            marker="o",
            linewidth=2,
            markersize=8,
            color="blue",
        )
        axes[0, 0].set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
        axes[0, 0].set_ylabel("Pass@1 Rate")
        axes[0, 0].set_title(
            f'Pass@1 Progression - {dataset.replace("_", " ").title()}'
        )
        axes[0, 0].grid(alpha=0.3)

        # Subplot 2: Improvement over baseline
        axes[0, 1].bar(range(len(improvements)), improvements, alpha=0.7, color="green")
        axes[0, 1].set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
        axes[0, 1].set_ylabel("Improvement over Baseline")
        axes[0, 1].set_title(
            f'Absolute Improvement - {dataset.replace("_", " ").title()}'
        )
        axes[0, 1].grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)
        axes[0, 1].axhline(y=0, color="black", linestyle="-", linewidth=0.8)

        # Subplot 3: Average stages used
        avg_stages = [predictions[c]["analysis"]["avg_stages"] for c in configs]
        axes[1, 0].plot(
            range(len(avg_stages)),
            avg_stages,
            marker="s",
            linewidth=2,
            markersize=8,
            color="purple",
        )
        axes[1, 0].set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
        axes[1, 0].set_ylabel("Average Refinement Stages")
        axes[1, 0].set_title(f'Stage Utilization - {dataset.replace("_", " ").title()}')
        axes[1, 0].grid(alpha=0.3)

        # Subplot 4: Loss curves
        train_losses = data["losses"]["train"]
        val_losses = data["losses"]["val"]
        axes[1, 1].plot(
            range(len(train_losses)),
            train_losses,
            marker="o",
            label="Train Loss",
            linewidth=2,
        )
        axes[1, 1].plot(
            range(len(val_losses)),
            val_losses,
            marker="s",
            label="Val Loss",
            linewidth=2,
        )
        axes[1, 1].set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
        axes[1, 1].set_ylabel("Loss (1 - Pass@1)")
        axes[1, 1].set_title(f'Loss Curves - {dataset.replace("_", " ").title()}')
        axes[1, 1].legend()
        axes[1, 1].grid(alpha=0.3)

        plt.suptitle(
            f'{dataset.replace("_", " ").title()} - Detailed Analysis (Single Run)',
            fontsize=14,
            fontweight="bold",
        )
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.savefig(
            os.path.join(working_dir, f"{dataset}_detailed_analysis.png"),
            dpi=150,
            bbox_inches="tight",
        )
        plt.close()
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error creating detailed analysis plot for {dataset}: {e}")
        plt.close()

# Plot 4: Correction rates by error category (heatmap-style)
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5))
    for idx, dataset in enumerate(datasets):
        data = experiment_data["single_run_evaluation"][dataset]
        stage_analysis = data["stage_analysis"]

        if stage_analysis and len(stage_analysis) > 0:
            categories = list(stage_analysis[-1]["correction_rates"].keys())
            if categories:
                correction_matrix = []
                for analysis in stage_analysis:
                    rates = [
                        analysis["correction_rates"].get(cat, 0) for cat in categories
                    ]
                    correction_matrix.append(rates)

                im = axes[idx].imshow(
                    np.array(correction_matrix).T,
                    aspect="auto",
                    cmap="YlGn",
                    vmin=0,
                    vmax=1,
                )
                axes[idx].set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
                axes[idx].set_ylabel("Error Category")
                axes[idx].set_title(f'{dataset.replace("_", " ").title()}')
                axes[idx].set_xticks(range(len(stage_analysis)))
                axes[idx].set_yticks(range(len(categories)))
                axes[idx].set_yticklabels(categories)

                for i in range(len(categories)):
                    for j in range(len(stage_analysis)):
                        text = axes[idx].text(
                            j,
                            i,
                            f"{correction_matrix[j][i]:.2f}",
                            ha="center",
                            va="center",
                            color="black",
                            fontsize=8,
                        )

    plt.suptitle(
        "Error Correction Rates by Category (Single Run)",
        fontsize=14,

plot_analyses.analysis: Pass@1 with standard deviation (No Error Boosting): Across all three datasets, Pass@1 varies only modestly across configurations C1–C4, and the error bars overlap substantially, suggesting differences may not be statistically meaningful without more runs. Synthetic code generation sits high (~0.91–0.95) with large variance (wide SD bars), indicating instability across seeds/problems; C4 is highest (~0.95) and C2 lowest (~0.91). Code contests is low (~0.369–0.375) with moderate SD; C2/C3 are slightly better than C1/C4. MBPP is high (~0.89–0.90) with small-to-moderate SD; C2/C4 are best (~0.90) while C3 dips (~0.89). Overall, removing “error boosting” yields a relatively flat Pass@1 landscape; any component differences are subtle at the Pass@1 level, especially given variance.

Average refinement stages utilized: Synthetic and MBPP are flat at exactly ~1.0 across all configurations, implying the refinement loop is effectively not being used (or always terminating after a single stage) for these datasets under the no-boost setting. Code contests shows a clear jump: C1 uses ~1.0 stages, while C2–C4 use ~1.62 stages on average, indicating that for harder tasks the system engages additional refinement when configured beyond C1. This suggests adaptive/refinement mechanisms only activate meaningfully on code_contests, and any ablation effects on stage selection will be most observable there.

Semantic error reduction rates across datasets: Synthetic shows very large reductions (~68–82%), with C4 highest (~82%) and C2 lowest (~68%). Code contests shows small reductions (~7.3–8.3%), with C2/C3 highest (~8.3%) and C1/C4 lower (~7.3%). MBPP shows modest reductions (~4.3–13%), with C2 and C4 highest (~13%), C1 ~8.7%, and C3 notably low (~4.3%). Key pattern: semantic error reduction is much more sensitive to configuration than Pass@1, especially on synthetic and MBPP. The mismatch (large error reduction but small Pass@1 changes) suggests many “semantic errors” being reduced may not be fully captured by the benchmark’s pass/fail tests, or improvements are concentrated in cases that still fail other requirements. C3 appears consistently weaker on MBPP semantic reduction, hinting that whatever differs in C3 harms semantic guidance for that dataset.

code_contests detailed panel (No Error Boosting): Pass@1 for ablation is ~0.369 (C1), ~0.375 (C2/C3), ~0.369 (C4), while the baseline dashed line is ~0.319—so all configurations outperform baseline by ~0.05–0.056 absolute. Semantic error reduction mirrors this: C2/C3 ~8.3% vs C1/C4 ~7.3%. Error correction by category: for logic errors, correction rates rise from ~0.75 (C1) to ~0.80 (C2) to ~0.89 (C3) but drop to ~0.67 (C4), suggesting C4 may trade off logic robustness. For algorithmic errors, all shown configurations are at ~1.0 (perfect), which is suspiciously saturated and may indicate either very small sample size or a metric definition that clips at 1.0; this needs verification. Stage utilization matches earlier plot: C1 ~1.0 vs C2–C4 ~1.62, implying the extra refinement stages correlate with the small gains in Pass@1 and semantic reduction on code_contests.

Ablation study: Pass@1 train vs val (No Error Boosting): Train and validation curves are essentially identical for all datasets and configurations, indicating either (a) evaluation is being run on the same split, (b) the metric is deterministic per configuration with no stochasticity, or (c) there is leakage/overlap or logging duplication. Synthetic: C4 best (~0.95), C2 worst (~0.913). Code contests: C2/C3 best (~0.375), C1/C4 lower (~0.369). MBPP: C2/C4 best (~0.90), C3 worst (~0.89). The near-perfect train/val overlap reduces confidence in generalization claims from this plot alone; you likely need separate held-out sets or multiple seeds to show robust differences.

Improvement over baseline across configurations: Improvements are reported as absolute deltas (not percentages). Synthetic shows large gains (~0.19–0.225), with C4 highest (~0.225) and C2 lowest (~0.188). Code contests gains are ~0.05–0.056, with C2/C3 slightly higher. MBPP gains are tiny (~0.005–0.015), with C2/C4 highest (~0.015) and C3 lowest (~0.005). This reinforces that (i) synthetic baseline is much weaker than the ablated system even without error boosting, (ii) code contests sees moderate gains, and (iii) MBPP baseline is already strong so headroom is limited; improvements there are primarily via semantic error reduction rather than large Pass@1 jumps.

mbpp detailed panel (No Error Boosting): Pass@1 ablation is ~0.895 (C1), ~0.900 (C2), ~0.890 (C3), ~0.900 (C4), baseline ~0.885—so gains are ~0.005–0.015 absolute. Error correction by category: algorithmic correction is ~0.5 for C1 but ~1.0 for C2–C4; this suggests C1 lacks some mechanism that helps algorithmic correctness on MBPP, while later configurations fix it. Stage utilization is flat at 1.0 across all configs, meaning these improvements occur without additional refinement stages—pointing to prompt/feedback content differences rather than iterative refinement depth.

synthetic_code_generation detailed panel (No Error Boosting): Pass@1 ablation is high (~0.912–0.95) and far above baseline (~0.725), giving very large absolute gains. Error correction rates by category (Best config): All shown categories are at 1.0 correction rate (Synthetic: algorithmic; Code Contests: algorithmic and logic; MBPP: algorithmic). This is suspiciously saturated and may reflect either (a) very small sample sizes per category, (b) a metric computed only on cases where correction was attempted, or (c) selection of a ‘best config’ that already filters to categories with perfect correction. As presented, it does not help discriminate component contributions; adding counts/denominators and confidence intervals would be necessary to interpret this meaningfully.

I will implement an ablation study that removes the stage-history tracking mechanism from the multi-stage refinement system. The key change is to modify the `multi_stage_refinement` function so that each refinement stage operates independently without access to information from previous stages (error categories, code changes, or patterns retrieved). This means removing the `stage_history` list that accumulates information across stages and ensuring that pattern retrieval and refinement decisions at each stage don't depend on what happened in earlier stages. The system will still perform multiple refinement attempts, but each stage will start fresh without memory of previous attempts within the same problem instance.

plot_plan: I'll create visualizations for this ablation study experiment that removes stage-history tracking and sequential learning. The experiment evaluates multi-stage code refinement across three datasets (synthetic_code_generation, code_contests, and mbpp) without using previous stage information. I'll plot training/validation metrics, error reduction rates, correction rates by category, and comparative analyses across datasets.

plot_analyses.analysis: Average refinement stages (No Stage History): Synthetic Code Generation stays flat at 1.0 across C1–C4, and MBPP also stays at 1.0, indicating the system effectively does a single-pass (no iterative refinement) under this ablation for these datasets. Code Contests increases from 1.0 at C1 to ~1.62 at C2 and remains ~1.62 through C4, suggesting harder tasks trigger additional refinement even without stage-history conditioning. This asymmetry implies stage history is not required to invoke extra refinement on Code Contests, but may be redundant/unused on easier datasets where the controller never escalates stages.

Pass@1 comparison across configurations (No Stage History): Synthetic Code Generation is high across all configs (~0.95, ~0.95, ~0.99, ~0.98) with noticeable variance (error bars), and peaks at C3. Code Contests is essentially constant at ~0.375 across C1–C4 with tiny/no visible variance, indicating configuration changes do not move the needle for this dataset under this ablation. MBPP is also essentially constant at ~0.90 across C1–C4 with very small variance. Overall, configuration choice matters most for Synthetic Code Generation, while Code Contests and MBPP appear bottlenecked elsewhere (e.g., missing capability, retrieval mismatch, or evaluation ceiling) such that these config tweaks don’t translate into pass@1 gains.

Semantic error reduction by dataset (No Stage History): Synthetic Code Generation shows large differences across configs: C1 and C2 at 81.8%, C3 at 95.5% (best), C4 at 90.9%. This aligns with the Pass@1 peak at C3, suggesting C3’s setting improves both correctness and semantic error avoidance. Code Contests is flat at 8.3% for all configs, and MBPP ranges from 8.7% (C1) to 13.0% (C2–C4). The flatness on Code Contests reinforces that the ablated system’s semantic guidance is not effectively addressing contest-style semantic failures; MBPP gets a modest boost after C1 but then saturates. The gap between Synthetic’s strong reductions and Code Contests’ weak reductions suggests dataset-specific failure modes not covered by the negative examples or feedback signal when stage history is removed.

Synthetic Code Generation detailed panels (No Stage History): Pass@1 for the ablation is ~0.95 (C1/C2), ~0.99 (C3), ~0.975 (C4), all well above the plotted baseline (~0.725), indicating this ablation variant still substantially outperforms the baseline. Semantic error reduction mirrors earlier plot: ~81–82% (C1/C2), ~95% (C3), ~91% (C4). Error correction by category shows only one visible category (“algorithmic”) with correction rate at 1.0 for C3 and C4, while C1/C2 are absent/0—this likely indicates either only algorithmic errors were tracked here or other categories had no counts; interpret cautiously. Stage utilization is exactly 1.0 across configs, confirming no multi-stage refinement is being used despite the ‘refinement’ framing—improvements are coming from prompt/config changes rather than iterative correction.

Pass@1 rate progression over configuration epochs (No Stage History): Synthetic Code Generation validation pass@1 is stable-high with a small bump around epoch 2 (~0.99) and slight dip at epoch 3 (~0.98). Code Contests train/val pass@1 is completely flat at ~0.375 across epochs, indicating no learning/progress from these configuration epochs (or that epochs represent config sweeps rather than training). MBPP shows a tiny improvement from ~0.895 to ~0.90 by epoch 1 and then plateaus. This suggests either (a) the system is not actually being trained here (just evaluated per config), or (b) the ablated signal provides little gradient/selection pressure for Code Contests; for MBPP/Synthetic, gains are marginal and saturate quickly.

MBPP detailed panels (No Stage History): Pass@1 improves slightly from ~0.895 (C1) to ~0.90 (C2–C4) and remains above the baseline (~0.885). Semantic error reduction increases from 8.7% (C1) to 13.0% (C2–C4), then saturates. Error correction by category shows “algorithmic” correction at 1.0 for all configs, which again suggests either a single tracked category or a plotting artifact; if true, it implies that when an algorithmic error is detected, the correction mechanism succeeds, but overall semantic error reduction remains modest because many errors are not being detected/targeted. Stage utilization is fixed at 1.0, consistent with no iterative refinement on MBPP under this ablation.

Code Contests detailed panels (No Stage History): Pass@1 is ~0.375 for all configs, above the baseline (~0.319) but with no sensitivity to configuration. Semantic error reduction is ~8.3% across all configs—very low and flat. Error correction by category shows logic correction varying (C1 ~0.75, C2/C4 1.0, C3 ~0.89) and algorithmic correction at 1.0 for C1 and C3 (others not shown/0). This mismatch—high per-category correction rates but low overall semantic error reduction—suggests either (i) corrections apply to a small subset of errors, (ii) category counts are small and unstable, or (iii) semantic error reduction metric is dominated by many uncategorized/undetected failures. Stage utilization increases to ~1.62 from C2 onward, indicating additional refinement attempts are made, yet they do not translate into higher pass@1 or semantic error reduction, pointing to ineffective feedback content (e.g., missing stage history removes crucial context for choosing the right fix) or to hard problems requiring deeper algorithmic changes beyond the refinement capability.

Training/validation loss (No Stage History): Synthetic Code Generation loss drops from ~0.05 (epochs 0–1) to ~0.0125 at epoch 2, then rises to ~0.025 at epoch 3; train and val curves overlap, suggesting either identical measurement or no separate split effect. This aligns with the pass@1 peak at epoch 2. Code Contests loss is perfectly flat at ~0.625 for both train and val across epochs, consistent with the flat pass@1 and indicating no improvement signal. MBPP loss drops from ~0.105 at epoch 0 to ~0.10 at epoch 1 and stays flat, matching the small pass@1 gain then plateau. Overall, the ablation ‘No Stage History’ seems to remove whatever mechanism would drive continued improvement on Code Contests, while Synthetic benefits from a particular configuration/epoch (epoch 2 / C3).

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots cover a ‘No Stage History’ ablation across C1–C4 for three datasets. Key patterns: (1) Stage utilization is 1.0 for Synthetic and MBPP (no iterative refinement), but ~1.62 for Code Contests from C2 onward (extra refinement without gains). (2) Pass@1 is high and config-sensitive only on Synthetic (best at C3/epoch2); MBPP and Code Contests are nearly flat across configs. (3) Semantic error reduction is strong on Synthetic (up to 95.5% at C3), modest on MBPP (up to 13%), and very low/flat on Code Contests (~8.3%). (4) Loss curves mirror these: Synthetic improves then slightly regresses; Code Contests is flat; MBPP improves slightly then plateaus.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")

try:
    experiment_data = np.load(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), allow_pickle=True
    ).item()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error loading experiment data: {e}")

# Extract data
ablation_data = experiment_data["ablation_uniform_pattern_retrieval"]
datasets = ["synthetic_code_generation", "code_contests", "mbpp"]

# Plot 1: Training/Validation Loss Curves for All Datasets
try:
    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 4))
    for idx, dataset in enumerate(datasets):
        train_loss = ablation_data[dataset]["losses"]["train"]
        val_loss = ablation_data[dataset]["losses"]["val"]
        epochs = range(len(train_loss))
        axes[idx].plot(epochs, train_loss, "o-", label="Train Loss", linewidth=2)
        axes[idx].plot(epochs, val_loss, "s-", label="Val Loss", linewidth=2)
        axes[idx].set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
        axes[idx].set_ylabel("Loss (1 - Pass@1)")
        axes[idx].set_title(f'{dataset.replace("_", " ").title()}')
        axes[idx].legend()
        axes[idx].grid(alpha=0.3)
    fig.suptitle(
        "Training and Validation Loss Curves - Uniform Random Pattern Retrieval",
        fontsize=14,
        fontweight="bold",
    )
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "loss_curves_all_datasets.png"),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating loss curves plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 2: Pass@1 Performance Comparison Across Datasets
try:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
    x = np.arange(4)
    width = 0.25
    for idx, dataset in enumerate(datasets):
        metrics = ablation_data[dataset]["metrics"]["val"]
        ax.bar(
            x + idx * width,

            width,

            alpha=0.8,

    ax.set_ylabel("Pass@1 Rate", fontsize=12)
    ax.set_title(
        "Pass@1 Performance Across Datasets and Configurations\nUniform Random Pattern Retrieval",
        fontsize=14,
        fontweight="bold",
    )
    ax.set_xticks(x + width)
    ax.set_xticklabels(["Config 1", "Config 2", "Config 3", "Config 4"])
    ax.legend()
    ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "pass_at_1_comparison_all_datasets.png"),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating Pass@1 comparison plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 3: Error Reduction Rates
try:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
    for idx, dataset in enumerate(datasets):
        error_reductions = ablation_data[dataset]["error_type_tracking"]
        ax.plot(
            range(len(error_reductions)),
            error_reductions,
            "o-",
            label=dataset.replace("_", " ").title(),
            linewidth=2,
            markersize=8,
        )
    ax.axhline(y=0, color="black", linestyle="--", linewidth=1)
    ax.set_xlabel("Configuration Index", fontsize=12)
    ax.set_ylabel("Error Reduction Rate (%)", fontsize=12)
    ax.set_title(
        "Semantic Error Reduction Rates Across Datasets\nUniform Random Pattern Retrieval",
        fontsize=14,
        fontweight="bold",
    )
    ax.legend()
    ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "error_reduction_rates_all_datasets.png"),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating error reduction plot: {e}")
    plt.close()

# Plot 4: Detailed Analysis for Synthetic Dataset
try:
    dataset = "synthetic_code_generation"
    predictions = ablation_data[dataset]["predictions"]
    config_keys = sorted([k for k in predictions.keys() if k.startswith("config_")])

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 10))

    # Mean scores with error bars
    means = [predictions[k]["mean"] for k in config_keys]
    stds = [predictions[k]["std"] for k in config_keys]
    axes[0, 0].errorbar(
        range(len(means)),
        means,
        yerr=stds,
        fmt="o-",
        linewidth=2,
        markersize=8,
        capsize=5,
    )
    axes[0, 0].set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
    axes[0, 0].set_ylabel("Pass@1 Rate")

    avg_stages = [predictions[k]["analysis"]["avg_stages"] for k in config_keys]

    axes[0, 1].set_ylabel("Average Stages")

    # Improvement over baseline

    axes[1, 0].bar(range(len(improvements)), improvements, alpha=0.7, color="green")

    axes[1, 0].set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
    axes[1, 0].set_ylabel("Improvement")
    axes[1, 0].set_title("Improvement Over Baseline - Synthetic")
    axes[1, 0].grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)

    # Error reduction
    error_red = [predictions[k]["semantic_error_reduction_rate"] for k in config_keys]
    axes[1, 1].plot(
        range(len(error_red)), error_red, "o-", linewidth=2, markersize=8, color="red"
    )
    axes[1, 1].set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
    axes[1, 1].set_ylabel("Error Reduction (%)")
    axes[1, 1].set_title("Semantic Error Reduction - Synthetic")
    axes[1, 1].grid(alpha=0.3)

    fig.suptitle(
        "Detailed Analysis: Synthetic Code Generation Dataset",
        fontsize=14,
        fontweight="bold",
    )
    plt.tight_layout()

        os.path.join(working_dir, "detailed_analysis_synthetic.png"),
        dpi=150,
        bbox_inches="tight",
    )
    plt.close()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error creating detailed analysis plot: {e}")

# Plot 5: Correction Rates by Error Category for MBPP
try:

plot_plan: This experiment evaluates an ablation study on code generation with uniform random pattern retrieval (removing keyword-based scoring). The experiment tracks Pass@1 rates, error reduction rates, and correction rates across multiple configurations and datasets (synthetic, code_contests, MBPP). I'll create visualizations showing performance metrics, training/validation curves, error correction analysis, and comparative results across the three datasets.

plot_analyses.analysis:
Training/validation loss (defined as 1−Pass@1) across configuration indices shows small, mostly monotonic improvements for Code Contests and MBPP, and a non-monotonic pattern for Synthetic.
- Synthetic: loss ~0.0375 at idx0, worsens to ~0.050 at idx1, then improves to ~0.025 at idx2/idx3. This suggests configuration 1 harms generalization (or retrieval noise), while later configs recover and outperform.
- Code Contests: loss drops from ~0.644 to ~0.625 by idx1 and then plateaus through idx3, indicating gains are achieved early and additional configuration changes don’t further help.
- MBPP: loss drops from ~0.105 to ~0.100 by idx1 and then plateaus, same “early gain then saturation” behavior.
- Train vs val curves are essentially overlapping (markers coincide), implying either identical values were logged/used for both or the model is not being trained in a way that differentiates train/val; this limits interpretability for overfitting/regularization claims.
Implication for the ablation stage: these plots look more like evaluation summaries per configuration than true epoch-wise learning curves; they can still support that uniform-random retrieval yields modest but saturating improvements, but they don’t isolate feedback/error-detection/adaptive-stage effects.

MBPP (Uniform Random) shows small Pass@1 gains over the baseline and modest semantic error reduction, with essentially fixed stage usage.
- Pass@1: baseline line is ~0.885. Configs: C1 ~0.895, C2–C4 ~0.900. That’s +0.010 to +0.015 absolute over baseline (≈+1.1% to +1.7% relative).
- Error reduction: C1 ~8.7%, C2–C4 ~13%. Improvement saturates after C2.
- Error correction by category: only “algorithmic” is shown and all configs are at ~1.0 correction rate, which conflicts with the modest overall error reduction; likely this panel is computed on a filtered subset (e.g., only instances where an algorithmic error was detected) or the metric is normalized differently.
- Stage utilization: flat at 1.0 average refinement stages for all configs, indicating the system is effectively single-pass/no refinement on MBPP under this setting. Therefore, improvements are attributable to prompt augmentation/retrieval content rather than multi-stage refinement/feedback loops.
Implication: uniform-random pattern retrieval provides limited but consistent benefit on MBPP; however, because refinement stages are not used, this plot cannot inform the “feedback mechanism” ablation directly—unless the feedback mechanism is what triggers additional stages, which appears inactive here.

Synthetic Code Generation detailed analysis indicates high Pass@1 with moderate variance, strong semantic error reduction, and no change in refinement stages.
- Pass@1 (with std): values ~0.962 (idx0), ~0.950 (idx1), ~0.975 (idx2/idx3). Error bars are large (down to ~0.85–0.90 and up to ~1.05), suggesting high variance or small sample size; differences between configs may not be statistically significant.
- Average refinement stages: constant at 1.0 across configs → again no effective multi-stage refinement.
- Improvement over baseline: bars ~0.225–0.25 (absolute). This is very large compared to the Pass@1 axis (~0.95–0.98), implying the baseline Pass@1 is around ~0.725 (consistent with the dashed baseline in other plots). So uniform-random retrieval yields a substantial absolute gain on this dataset.
- Semantic error reduction: ~86% (idx0), dips to ~82% (idx1), then rises to ~91% (idx2/idx3). This mirrors the loss plot non-monotonicity and suggests configuration 1 introduces noisier or less relevant negatives.
Implication: synthetic tasks benefit strongly from even random failure-pattern negatives, possibly because failure modes are repetitive and easy to match; but configuration sensitivity (idx1) suggests retrieval/prompt formatting details matter.

Cross-dataset semantic error reduction under uniform-random retrieval shows strong dataset dependence and early saturation.
- Synthetic: very high error reduction (~82–91%), with best at idx2/idx3 (~91%).
- MBPP: low-to-moderate (~9–13%), plateau after idx1.
- Code Contests: low (~7–10%), plateau after idx1.
This indicates that random negative demonstrations help most when failure modes are homogeneous (synthetic), but are much less effective on diverse, long-horizon problems (Code Contests) and only modestly helpful on MBPP.
Implication for FACG: retrieval quality likely becomes the bottleneck on real benchmarks; uniform-random negatives are insufficient, motivating the proposed similarity-based retrieval and clustering into interpretable failure modes.

MBPP error correction rates by category (algorithmic only) show high correction across configs.
- Config 1 ~0.95, Config 2 ~0.90, Config 3/4 ~1.0.
Given overall MBPP error reduction is only ~9–13%, this likely reflects a conditional metric (e.g., correction rate among detected algorithmic-error cases) rather than global correction.
Implication: you need to report denominators (#cases per category) and whether ‘correction’ is measured after a refinement step or via single-pass avoidance. As plotted, it overstates performance and is hard to reconcile with the aggregate error reduction.

Code Contests (Uniform Random) shows small Pass@1 improvements and modest error reduction, with increased refinement stage usage in later configs.
- Pass@1: baseline ~0.306; C1 ~0.356; C2–C4 ~0.375. That’s +0.050 to +0.069 absolute (≈+16% to +23% relative), which is meaningful.
- Error reduction: C1 ~7.2%, C2–C4 ~9.9% (plateau).
- Error correction by category: only “logic” shown; correction rates vary (C1 ~0.8, C2 ~0.9, C3 ~0.75, C4 ~1.0). This volatility suggests small counts and/or sensitivity to prompt variants.

Implication for the ablation goals: Code Contests is the best dataset to quantify feedback/adaptive-stage contributions because stage utilization changes. However, the current plots are for “uniform random pattern retrieval” rather than explicit component removal; you’ll need matched ablations (feedback off, error detection off, adaptive off) to attribute the stage-utilization increase to specific components.

Synthetic Code Generation (Uniform Random) shows high Pass@1 and strong error reduction, but no refinement-stage usage.

This supports the paper’s core claim (proactive prevention), but it also means feedback-mechanism ablations won’t show much on this dataset unless you force multi-stage refinement to activate.

Pass@1 across datasets and configs under uniform-random retrieval highlights: (i) synthetic is near-solved, (ii) MBPP is high but saturating, (iii) Code Contests remains the bottleneck.

- MBPP: ~0.90 across configs (small spread).
- Code Contests: ~0.356→0.375, still low in absolute terms.
Config effects are small after C2 (saturation), suggesting that under uniform-random retrieval, most gains come from simply adding any negative/contrastive signal; further configuration tweaks don’t matter much.
Implication: to meet the sub-stage goal of ranking component importance, you’ll need stronger perturbations (true ablations) and/or better retrieval than uniform random to create separable effects, especially on MBPP where stage utilization is constant.

(4) Several category-level correction plots show only a single category and appear inconsistent with aggregate error reduction, suggesting conditional metrics or small-sample artifacts; denominators and definitions need clarification. Overall, uniform-random negatives help, but real-benchmark gains are limited, motivating accurate retrieval and component-wise ablations to attribute improvements to feedback, error detection, and adaptive stage selection.

plot_plan: This experiment evaluates a multi-stage code refinement approach on a unified dataset combining synthetic, code_contests, and MBPP problems. The system builds a failure pattern library from common errors and uses it to iteratively refine buggy code through multiple stages. Different configurations vary the number of refinement stages, retrieval parameters, and success rates. The code tracks pass@1 rates, semantic error reductions, error correction rates by category, and stage utilization across configurations to demonstrate the effectiveness of the multi-stage refinement approach.

plot_analyses.analysis: Average refinement stage utilization increases from Config 1 (~1.00) to Config 2 (~1.23) and peaks at Config 3 (~1.27), then slightly decreases at Config 4 (~1.23). This suggests the configuration changes between C2→C3 encourage the system to invoke additional refinement more often, while C4 achieves similar performance with slightly fewer stages than C3. The non-monotonicity (C3 > C4) is consistent with a more efficient feedback/refinement policy in C4 (or a stricter stopping criterion) that reduces stage usage without sacrificing accuracy.

Pass@1 (with error bars) increases modestly across configurations: C1 ~0.707, C2 ~0.716, C3 ~0.721, C4 ~0.727. The orange “Improvement over baseline” bars also rise from ~0.048 (C1) to ~0.068 (C4), indicating that later configurations deliver larger absolute gains over the baseline. Error bars overlap across configs, so the incremental gains (especially C2→C3) may be small relative to variance; however, the trend is consistently upward, suggesting a real but modest effect. If these configs correspond to enabling components (e.g., feedback, error detection, adaptive selection), the plot implies each added component yields diminishing but positive returns.

Semantic error reduction increases monotonically with configuration: 14.0% (C1), 16.7% (C2), 18.0% (C3), 20.0% (C4). This indicates later configurations are better at preventing/repairing semantic mistakes, aligning with the FACG hypothesis that stronger failure-aware guidance and/or feedback mechanisms reduce semantic errors. The gains are relatively steady (+2–3 percentage points per step), suggesting each incremental component contributes meaningfully to semantic correctness, not just superficial pass@1 improvements.

Training and validation loss (defined as 1 − Pass@1) decrease from config index 0 to 3, roughly from ~0.293 to ~0.273. The training and validation curves are nearly identical at each point, indicating minimal generalization gap and suggesting the improvements are not due to overfitting. The monotonic loss decrease matches the monotonic pass@1 increase, reinforcing that later configurations are genuinely better rather than trading off validation performance.

The composite figure reiterates key relationships: (i) Pass@1 vs refinement stages shows a monotonic increase from C1 to C4, while the baseline line sits lower (~0.659). This indicates multi-stage refinement consistently outperforms baseline prompting and that additional/optimized refinement correlates with higher pass@1. (ii) Semantic error reduction again increases from 14% to 20%, consistent with improved semantic robustness. (iii) Error correction by category shows heterogeneous effects: for ‘logic’, C2 is lowest (~0.67), C3 improves (~0.8), and C1/C4 are highest (~1.0). For ‘algorithmic’, C1 and C2 are highest (~1.0), C3 dips (~0.67), and C4 returns to ~1.0. This pattern suggests C3 may introduce a tradeoff or retrieval mismatch that harms algorithmic corrections while helping logic, whereas C4 resolves both—evidence of interaction effects between components (e.g., adaptive stage selection + feedback) that restore balanced correction across error types. (iv) Stage utilization mirrors the earlier stage plot: C3 uses the most stages, C4 slightly fewer, suggesting C4 is more efficient.

Training and validation Pass@1 curves overlap closely and increase from ~0.707 (index 0) to ~0.727 (index 3). The near-perfect overlap again indicates stability and little overfitting. The total gain (~+0.02 absolute) is modest but consistent; in ablation terms, this magnitude can be used to quantify the cumulative contribution of the added components represented by later configurations.

vlm_feedback_summary: Six plots were received for a unified dataset comparison across four configurations (C1–C4). Overall trends are consistent: later configurations improve Pass@1, reduce loss, and increase semantic error reduction. Refinement stage usage rises from C1 to C3 and slightly drops at C4, suggesting C4 achieves best accuracy with improved efficiency. Category-wise correction rates indicate potential interaction effects: C3 appears to underperform on algorithmic corrections while improving logic, whereas C4 performs strongly on both, implying synergistic component integration in the final configuration.

Ablation name: Remove Error-Category-Specific Failure Library Partitioning and Use Unified Pattern Pool.
The ablation study removes the error-category-specific partitioning of the failure library (the `defaultdict` structure organizing patterns by categories like "syntax", "logic", "boundary", etc.) and replaces it with a single unified pool of all failure patterns. Instead of using `retrieve_failure_patterns_by_category()` which retrieves patterns from a specific error category, we'll create a new `retrieve_failure_patterns_unified()` function that retrieves patterns from a flat list using only keyword-based similarity scoring, ignoring error categories entirely. This tests whether the fine-grained categorization provides value or if simpler keyword-based retrieval is sufficient. We'll compare the ablation's performance against the baseline to measure the impact of removing category-specific organization.

Error correction by category: for “logic”, correction varies (≈0.75 at C1, 1.0 at C2, ≈0.89 at C3, 1.0 at C4), while for “algorithmic” it is 1.0 for C1, C3, C4 (C2 missing/0). This inconsistency suggests sparse counts per category or instability in how categories are measured. Stage utilization jumps from 1.0 (C1) to ≈1.62 (C2–C4) and then plateaus, implying later configs invoke additional refinement stages, yet this does not translate into higher pass@1—pointing to diminishing returns or ineffective refinement on harder contest problems (possibly due to weak error detection signals, long-horizon reasoning limits, or retrieval mismatch).

Unified Pattern Pool Ablation: Pass@1 across configuration epochs: Synthetic shows clear improvement in validation (≈0.95 at epochs 0–1 → ≈0.988 at epoch 2 → 1.0 at epoch 3), indicating the configuration progression meaningfully improves generalization. Code Contests validation is flat at ≈0.375 across epochs, matching the earlier ablation panel and reinforcing that the current changes do not affect contest performance. MBPP validation improves slightly from ≈0.895 at epoch 0 to ≈0.900 by epoch 1 and then saturates, indicating early gains and quick convergence.

Unified Pattern Pool training/validation loss (defined as 1 - pass@1): Synthetic loss drops from ≈0.05 (epochs 0–1) to ≈0.0125 (epoch 2) to 0 (epoch 3), consistent with the pass@1 curve and suggesting near-perfect performance by the final config. Code Contests loss is constant at ≈0.625 across epochs, again showing no learning/effect from configuration changes. MBPP loss decreases from ≈0.105 to ≈0.10 at epoch 1 and stays flat, mirroring the small pass@1 gain and saturation.

Unified Pattern Pool stage utilization across configurations: Synthetic and MBPP are flat at 1.0 across all configurations, while Code Contests increases from 1.0 at config 0 to ≈1.62 at config 1 and remains there. This indicates adaptive/refinement behavior is only being triggered for contest tasks, but the extra stages are not improving pass@1—suggesting either refinement is not targeted (weak feedback), or the bottleneck is initial solution quality/retrieval rather than iterative correction.

Unified Pattern Pool semantic error reduction rates across configurations: Synthetic increases strongly with configuration (≈81% at config 0/1 → ≈95% at config 2 → 100% at config 3), matching the earlier synthetic ablation. Code Contests is flat at ≈8.2% across all configs, again indicating insensitivity. MBPP increases from ≈8.7% at config 0 to ≈13% at configs 1–3 and then plateaus, suggesting most benefit comes from the first configuration change; later changes do not add additional error prevention.

Performance improvement by configuration (over baseline): Synthetic shows large gains that increase with configuration (≈+22.5% at configs 1/2 → ≈+26% at config 3 → ≈+27.5% at config 4), indicating later configs add meaningful benefit. Code Contests shows a constant ≈+5.6% improvement across all configs, consistent with flat pass@1. MBPP shows small improvements (≈+1.0% at config 1, ≈+1.5% at configs 2–4), consistent with minor pass@1 gains and early saturation. Overall, dataset sensitivity differs sharply: synthetic is highly responsive to configuration changes; contests is not; MBPP is mildly responsive.

mbpp ablation panel: Pass@1 for unified pool is ≈0.895 at C1 and ≈0.900 for C2–C4, above the baseline ≈0.885. Error reduction increases from ≈8.7% (C1) to ≈13% (C2–C4) and then saturates, indicating the key component change occurs between C1 and C2. Error correction by category shows only “algorithmic” with correction rate 1.0 for all configs, which may indicate that when algorithmic errors are detected they are fully corrected, but overall error reduction remains modest—suggesting many remaining failures are outside this category or not being corrected. Stage utilization is flat at 1.0 across configs, implying improvements come from better initial generation guidance rather than additional refinement stages.

This pattern suggests the varied component(s) improve initial semantic guidance on easier/structured tasks but do not translate into better contest performance; refinement appears to add compute without gains on contests, indicating a bottleneck in retrieval/feedback quality or difficulty/coverage of failure patterns for that dataset.

import numpy as np

ablation_types = ["context_aware_retrieval", "random_retrieval"]

    fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5))
    for idx, dataset in enumerate(datasets):
        ax = axes[idx]
        for ablation in ablation_types:
            metrics = experiment_data[ablation][dataset]["metrics"]["val"]

                "Context-Aware" if ablation == "context_aware_retrieval" else "Random"

            color = "blue" if ablation == "context_aware_retrieval" else "red"

                color=color,
                linewidth=2,

            fontweight="bold",

        ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
