plot_plan: I'll create visualizations for the global error aggregation ablation experiment. The code examines how a unified failure library (pooling errors from all datasets) performs across different configurations, tracking Pass@1 rates, error reduction, correction rates by error category, and refinement stage utilization.

plot_analyses.analysis: Across synthetic_code_generation, Pass@1 under global aggregation is very high and increases slightly with later configurations: ~0.95 at C1/C2 and ~0.99 at C3/C4, while the dashed baseline is much lower (~0.725). Error reduction similarly rises from ~81–82% (C1/C2) to ~95% (C3/C4). Stage utilization is flat at ~1.0 for all configurations, indicating these gains are not coming from using more refinement stages but from configuration changes themselves. The ‘Global Error Correction’ panel shows algorithmic correction rate at 1.0 for C3/C4 (and effectively absent/0 for C1/C2), suggesting that whatever component is introduced by C3/C4 is what enables full correction on the tracked category; however, the categorical plot looks sparse and may reflect limited logging rather than true absence of other error types.

Average refinement stages (global aggregation) differ sharply by dataset: synthetic and MBPP sit exactly at 1.0 across all configurations (no extra refinement), while code_contests jumps from 1.0 at configuration 0 to ~1.62 at configuration 1 and stays there through configuration 3. This implies adaptive staging/feedback triggers additional refinement only for the harder code_contests tasks, while the other datasets are solved (or evaluated) in a single pass regardless of configuration. For ablation interpretation, this means any component differences for synthetic/MBPP are unlikely to be mediated by ‘more iterations’ and instead by prompt/content changes; for code_contests, iteration count is a confounder and should be controlled when attributing gains to specific components.

Semantic error reduction rates (global aggregation) show strong dataset dependence and modest sensitivity to configuration. Synthetic code generation improves from ~82% at configuration 0/1 to ~95% at configuration 2/3. Code_contests is essentially flat at ~8.2–8.3% across all configurations, indicating the tested configuration changes do not meaningfully reduce semantic errors there (or the metric is saturated/noisy). MBPP increases from ~8.7% at configuration 0 to ~13% at configuration 1 and then plateaus through configuration 3. This pattern suggests: (i) the component introduced between configs 1→2 matters a lot for synthetic, (ii) the component introduced between 0→1 matters for MBPP, and (iii) code_contests needs different mechanisms (better retrieval, longer context, or stronger feedback) to move semantic correctness.

For code_contests, Pass@1 under global aggregation is ~0.375 for all configurations (C1–C4), while the baseline line is ~0.319. Error reduction is also flat at ~8.25% across configurations. Stage utilization increases from 1.0 at C1 to ~1.62 at C2–C4, yet this extra refinement does not translate into higher Pass@1 or error reduction, suggesting the refinement loop is either not effective on this dataset or is being applied but not producing executable/semantically improved solutions. The ‘Global Error Correction’ by category shows high correction rates: logic ~0.75 (C1), 1.0 (C2), ~0.89 (C3), 1.0 (C4); algorithmic appears 1.0 for C3/C4 (and possibly C1) with C2 missing/0. The mismatch between high per-category correction and flat overall Pass@1 suggests either (a) correction rate is computed on a subset (only when an error is detected), (b) category labels are incomplete, or (c) corrections fix detected errors but introduce others that still fail tests.

For MBPP, Pass@1 is ~0.895 at C1 and ~0.900 at C2–C4; baseline is ~0.885. Error reduction increases from ~8.7% (C1) to ~13% (C2–C4). Global error correction for the algorithmic category is 1.0 for all configurations, and stage utilization is fixed at 1.0, indicating improvements are small and not driven by iterative refinement. The step change between C1→C2 suggests a single component addition (e.g., feedback mechanism or retrieval quality) yields the gain, after which further changes do not help MBPP.

Pass@1 train vs validation across configurations are nearly identical (overlapping) for all datasets, indicating little to no generalization gap under this evaluation setup. Synthetic shows a step from ~0.95 at configs 0/1 to ~0.987 at configs 2/3; code_contests is flat at ~0.375; MBPP steps from ~0.895 at config 0 to ~0.900 at configs 1–3. This reinforces that improvements are configuration-threshold effects rather than gradual trends. It also suggests either the ‘train’ split is not a training metric but an evaluation on a training subset, or the system is not being optimized in a way that would create divergence—important to clarify in the paper.

Improvement over baseline (global error aggregation) is largest for synthetic (~+0.225 at configs 0/1, ~+0.262 at configs 2/3), moderate for code_contests (~+0.056 constant), and small for MBPP (~+0.010 at config 0, ~+0.015 at configs 1–3). The constancy for code_contests again indicates configuration changes are not affecting outcomes there; the synthetic jump at configs 2/3 matches the Pass@1 and semantic error reduction jump, pointing to a key component becoming active at that point.

Final configuration correction rates by error category are shown as 1.0 for all displayed categories across all datasets (synthetic: algorithmic; code_contests: algorithmic and logic; MBPP: algorithmic). This is suspiciously perfect and inconsistent with non-perfect Pass@1, implying the ‘correction rate’ metric is likely conditional (e.g., among detected/categorized errors only) or computed on a small subset where correction was attempted. For ablation claims, this plot should be accompanied by counts/coverage: number of instances per category, fraction of failures categorized, and how ‘correction’ is defined (fixing the error signature vs passing all tests). Otherwise it risks overstating effectiveness.

vlm_feedback_summary: Plots cover Pass@1, semantic error reduction, per-category correction, and stage utilization across configurations C1–C4 (and numeric configs 0–3) for synthetic_code_generation, code_contests, and MBPP under global error aggregation. Main visible effects: synthetic improves markedly at later configs (Pass@1 ~0.95→~0.99; semantic error reduction ~82%→~95%) without increased stages; MBPP shows small step improvement at early configs (Pass@1 ~0.895→~0.900; error reduction ~8.7%→~13%) with stages fixed at 1; code_contests is flat in Pass@1 (~0.375) and error reduction (~8.25%) despite higher average stages (~1.62) in later configs. Several correction-rate plots show near- or exactly-1.0 values, suggesting conditional metrics or limited coverage and warranting reporting of denominators and definitions.

Ablation name: Remove Dynamic Refinement Success Rate and Use Stage-Independent Success Rate.
I'll implement the ablation study that removes the dynamic pattern-boosted refinement success rate and replaces it with a fixed, stage-independent success rate. This removes the line `refinement_success_rate *= 1.0 + 0.1 * len(relevant_patterns)` that adapts based on pattern retrieval quality. The ablation maintains all other components (failure library, pattern retrieval, error classification, multi-stage structure) but tests whether the adaptive mechanism actually contributes to performance or if gains come purely from the multi-stage structure itself.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import os

working_dir = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "working")

try:
    experiment_data = np.load(
        os.path.join(working_dir, "experiment_data.npy"), allow_pickle=True
    ).item()
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error loading experiment data: {e}")
    experiment_data = None

if experiment_data:
    datasets = ["synthetic_code_generation", "code_contests", "mbpp"]

    # Plot 1: Training and Validation Loss Curves for All Datasets
    try:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(16, 10))
        for idx, dataset in enumerate(datasets):
            baseline_train_loss = experiment_data["baseline"][dataset]["losses"][
                "train"
            ]
            baseline_val_loss = experiment_data["baseline"][dataset]["losses"]["val"]
            ablation_train_loss = experiment_data["ablation"][dataset]["losses"][
                "train"
            ]
            ablation_val_loss = experiment_data["ablation"][dataset]["losses"]["val"]

            ax_train = axes[0, idx]
            ax_train.plot(
                baseline_train_loss, "o-", label="Baseline", linewidth=2, markersize=6
            )
            ax_train.plot(
                ablation_train_loss, "s-", label="Ablation", linewidth=2, markersize=6
            )
            ax_train.set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
            ax_train.set_ylabel("Training Loss")
            ax_train.set_title(f'{dataset.replace("_", " ").title()}\nTraining Loss')
            ax_train.legend()
            ax_train.grid(alpha=0.3)

            ax_val = axes[1, idx]
            ax_val.plot(
                baseline_val_loss, "o-", label="Baseline", linewidth=2, markersize=6
            )
            ax_val.plot(
                ablation_val_loss, "s-", label="Ablation", linewidth=2, markersize=6
            )
            ax_val.set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
            ax_val.set_ylabel("Validation Loss")
            ax_val.set_title(f'{dataset.replace("_", " ").title()}\nValidation Loss')
            ax_val.legend()
            ax_val.grid(alpha=0.3)

        plt.suptitle(
            "Training and Validation Loss: Baseline vs Ablation Across Datasets",
            fontsize=14,
            fontweight="bold",
        )
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.savefig(
            os.path.join(working_dir, "loss_curves_all_datasets.png"),
            dpi=150,
            bbox_inches="tight",
        )
        plt.close()
        print("Created loss curves visualization")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error creating loss curves: {e}")
        plt.close()

    # Plot 2: Pass@1 Metrics Comparison
    try:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 5))
        for idx, dataset in enumerate(datasets):
            baseline_metrics = experiment_data["baseline"][dataset]["metrics"]["val"]
            ablation_metrics = experiment_data["ablation"][dataset]["metrics"]["val"]

            ax = axes[idx]
            x = np.arange(len(baseline_metrics))
            width = 0.35
            ax.bar(
                x - width / 2,
                baseline_metrics,
                width,
                label="Baseline (Pattern-Boosted)",
                alpha=0.8,
            )
            ax.bar(
                x + width / 2,
                ablation_metrics,
                width,
                label="Ablation (Fixed Rate)",
                alpha=0.8,
            )
            ax.set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
            ax.set_ylabel("Pass@1 Rate")
            ax.set_title(f'{dataset.replace("_", " ").title()}\nPass@1 Performance')
            ax.set_xticks(x)
            ax.legend()
            ax.grid(axis="y", alpha=0.3)

        plt.suptitle(
            "Pass@1 Performance: Baseline vs Ablation", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold"
        )
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.savefig(
            os.path.join(working_dir, "pass_at_1_comparison_all_datasets.png"),
            dpi=150,
            bbox_inches="tight",
        )
        plt.close()
        print("Created Pass@1 comparison visualization")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error creating Pass@1 comparison: {e}")
        plt.close()

    # Plot 3: Error Reduction Rates
    try:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(16, 5))
        for idx, dataset in enumerate(datasets):
            baseline_reductions = experiment_data["baseline"][dataset][
                "error_type_tracking"
            ]
            ablation_reductions = experiment_data["ablation"][dataset][
                "error_type_tracking"
            ]

            ax = axes[idx]
            x = np.arange(len(baseline_reductions))
            ax.plot(
                x,
                baseline_reductions,
                "o-",
                label="Baseline",
                linewidth=2,
                markersize=8,
            )
            ax.plot(
                x,
                ablation_reductions,
                "s-",
                label="Ablation",
                linewidth=2,
                markersize=8,
            )
            ax.set_xlabel("Configuration Index")
            ax.set_ylabel("Error Reduction Rate (%)")
            ax.set_title(
                f'{dataset.replace("_", " ").title()}\nSemantic Error Reduction'
            )
            ax.legend()
            ax.grid(alpha=0.3)

        plt.suptitle(
            "Semantic Error Reduction Rates Across Configurations",
            fontsize=14,
            fontweight="bold",
        )
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.savefig(
            os.path.join(working_dir, "error_reduction_rates_all_datasets.png"),
            dpi=150,
            bbox_inches="tight",
        )
        plt.close()
        print("Created error reduction visualization")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error creating error reduction plot: {e}")
        plt.close()

    # Plot 4: Performance Gap Analysis (Baseline - Ablation)
    try:
        fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))
        for dataset in datasets:
            baseline_metrics = experiment_data["baseline"][dataset]["metrics"]["val"]
            ablation_metrics = experiment_data["ablation"][dataset]["metrics"]["val"]
            performance_gap = [
                b - a for b, a in zip(baseline_metrics, ablation_metrics)
            ]
            ax.plot(
                performance_gap,
                "o-",
                label=dataset.replace("_", " ").title(),
                linewidth=2,
                markersize=8,
            )

        ax.axhline(y=0, color="black", linestyle="--", linewidth=1.5, alpha=0.5)
        ax.set_xlabel("Configuration Index", fontsize=12)
        ax.set_ylabel("Performance Gap (Baseline - Ablation)", fontsize=12)
        ax.set_title(
            "Pattern-Boosting Impact: Performance Gap Analysis\n(Positive = Baseline Better)",
            fontsize=13,
            fontweight="bold",
        )
        ax.legend()
        ax.grid(alpha=0.3)
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.savefig(
            os.path.join(working_dir, "performance_gap_analysis.png"),
            dpi=150,
            bbox_inches="tight",
        )
        plt.close()
        print("Created performance gap analysis")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error creating performance gap plot: {e}")
        plt.close()

    # Plot 5: Summary Statistics Heatmap
    try:
        fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
        summary_data = []
        row_labels = []

        for dataset in datasets:
            baseline_best = max(experiment_data["baseline"][dataset]["metrics"]["val"])
            ablation_best = max(experiment_data["ablation"][dataset]["metrics"]["val"])
            baseline_avg = np.mean(
                experiment_data["baseline"][dataset]["metrics"]["val"]
            )
            ablation_avg = np.mean(
                experiment_data["ablation"][dataset]["metrics"]["val"]
            )
            gap_best = baseline_best - ablation_best
            gap_avg = baseline_avg - ablation_avg

            summary_data.append(
                [
                    baseline_best,
                    ablation_best,
                    gap_best,
                    baseline_avg,
                    ablation_avg,
                    gap_avg,
                ]
            )
            row_labels.append(dataset.replace("_", " ").title())

        summary_data = np.array(summary_data)
        im = ax.imshow(summary_data, cmap="RdYlGn", aspect="auto", vmin=0, vmax=1)

        ax.set_xticks(np.arange(6))
        ax.set_yticks(np.arange(len(datasets)))
        ax.set_xticklabels(
            [
                "Baseline\nBest",
                "Ablation\nBest",
                "Gap\nBest",
                "Baseline\nAvg",
                "Ablation\nAvg",
                "Gap\nAvg",
            ],
            fontsize=9,
        )
        ax.set_yticklabels(row_labels)

        for i in range(len(datasets)):
            for j in range(6):
                text = ax.text(
                    j,
                    i,
                    f"{summary_data[i, j]:.3f}",
                    ha="center",
                    va="center",
                    color="black",
                    fontsize=10,
                    fontweight="bold",
                )

        ax.set_title(
            "Performance Summary: Baseline vs Ablation", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold"
        )
        plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, label="Pass@1 Rate")
        plt.tight_layout()
        plt.savefig(
            os.path.join(working_dir, "summary_heatmap.png"),
            dpi=150,
            bbox_inches="tight",
        )
        plt.close()
        print("Created summary heatmap")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error creating summary heatmap: {e}")
        plt.close()

print("All visualizations completed")
plot_plan: This experiment compares a baseline multi-stage code refinement system (where retrieved failure patterns boost refinement success rates) against an ablation where refinement success is fixed regardless of patterns retrieved. I'll create visualizations showing training/validation metrics across configurations, error reduction rates, performance comparisons between baseline and ablation for each dataset, and summary statistics demonstrating whether the pattern-boosting mechanism provides meaningful gains or if improvements come solely from the multi-stage structure.

plot_analyses.analysis:
Pattern-Boosting Impact: Performance Gap Analysis (Positive = Baseline Better)
- The plot shows Pass@1 gaps (Baseline − Ablation) across 4 configurations (indices 0–3) for three datasets.
- Synthetic code generation: gap is ~0.0125 at indices 0–1, then increases to ~0.025 at indices 2–3. This indicates the baseline (pattern-boosted) consistently outperforms the ablation, with the largest benefit in later configurations (likely higher-stage/stronger settings).
- Code contests: gap starts highest (~0.025) at index 0, drops to ~0.006 at index 1, and becomes 0 at indices 2–3. Interpretation: pattern-boosting helps mainly in the weakest/earliest configuration; by later configs the ablation catches up, suggesting either (i) the fixed-rate ablation is sufficient once enough refinement is allowed, or (ii) the contest set is bottlenecked by other components (e.g., error detection/adaptive stages) rather than pattern guidance.
- MBPP: gap decreases from ~0.015 (index 0) to ~0.01 (index 1) to 0 (indices 2–3). Similar to contests but less extreme: pattern-boosting helps early, but the advantage vanishes when using later configurations.
- Cross-dataset takeaway: pattern-boosting’s marginal value is configuration-dependent and largest where the system is otherwise weakest (early configs) for MBPP/contests, but remains material even in later configs for synthetic, implying synthetic tasks are more sensitive to the negative-pattern guidance signal.
- For the stated ablation stage (“Feedback Mechanism”), this plot likely corresponds to a specific component removal (or fixed-rate vs pattern-boosted). If this is indeed feedback ablation, then feedback seems most crucial for synthetic and less so for contests/MBPP once later configurations are enabled.

Training and Validation Loss: Baseline vs Ablation Across Datasets
- Six subplots: training loss (top row) and validation loss (bottom row) for synthetic, code contests, MBPP across configuration indices 0–3.
- Synthetic: baseline loss is consistently lower than ablation at all indices. Baseline drops sharply at index 2 (~0.012–0.013) then rises slightly at index 3 (~0.025). Ablation also drops at index 2 (~0.038) but stays substantially higher and rises to ~0.05 at index 3. Validation mirrors training almost exactly, suggesting stable optimization and no obvious overfitting differences; the ablation appears to reduce learning efficacy (worse fit) rather than just generalization.
- Code contests: baseline training/val loss is flat at ~0.625 across all indices. Ablation starts higher (~0.65 at index 0), improves to ~0.631 at index 1, and matches baseline (~0.625) at indices 2–3. This aligns with the performance-gap plot: the ablation disadvantage disappears in later configurations.
- MBPP: baseline training/val loss improves from ~0.105 (idx0) to ~0.10 (idx1–3). Ablation starts worse (~0.12), improves to ~0.11 (idx1), then matches baseline (~0.10) at indices 2–3. Again consistent with diminishing performance gap.
- Interpretation: the ablated component primarily affects optimization/learning signal on synthetic tasks across all configs, but for contests/MBPP the system can compensate by later configurations (perhaps more refinement stages or stronger prompting), leading to converged losses.
- Methodological note: losses being identical between train and val curves at each index suggests these may be aggregated/rounded summary values rather than epoch-wise curves; still, the relative ordering is informative for component contribution.

code_contests: Pass@1 Comparison / Error Reduction / Performance Impact
- Pass@1 line plot: baseline (pattern-boosted) is flat at 0.375 across C1–C4. Ablation rises from 0.35 (C1) to ~0.369 (C2) and reaches 0.375 at C3–C4. So best Pass@1 is identical (0.375) for both, but baseline is more robust at the earliest configuration.
- Error reduction bar plot: baseline is ~8.2% across configurations (flat). Ablation increases with configuration: ~6.3% (C1) → ~9.0% (C2) → ~9.9% (C3–C4). Thus, while Pass@1 converges, ablation can yield higher semantic error reduction at later configs, suggesting it may be “cleaning up” errors without translating to more solved problems (possibly because remaining failures are hard/algorithmic rather than semantic).
- Performance impact bar/summary: Pass@1 difference (baseline − ablation) is 0.025 at C1, ~0.006 at C2, and 0 at C3–C4. Text summary reports max diff 0.025 and avg diff 0.008, i.e., ~0.8% average gain for baseline. This quantifies that pattern-boosting is modest but meaningful mainly for low-refinement settings.
- Implication for component ablation goals: code_contests appears least sensitive in final performance to this component (best Pass@1 unchanged), but sensitive in early-stage robustness. If your paper emphasizes single-pass generation, highlight C1/C2 where the gap exists; if multi-stage allowed, the benefit may be negligible on contests.

Semantic Error Reduction Rates Across Configurations
- Synthetic: baseline semantic error reduction is high and peaks at index 2 (~95–96%), with ~82% at indices 0–1 and ~91% at index 3. Ablation is consistently lower: ~74% at indices 0–1, ~84% at index 2, ~79% at index 3. Large consistent gap indicates the ablated component materially improves semantic correctness on synthetic tasks.
- Code contests: baseline is flat at ~8.25% across indices. Ablation starts lower (~6.3%) but surpasses baseline at indices 1–3 (~9.0% then ~9.9%). This inversion suggests the ablation may change error profile: fewer “semantic” errors as defined by your metric, but not enough to improve pass@1 beyond baseline ceiling. Alternatively, baseline may already be saturated on this metric due to limited detectable semantic errors in contest problems.
- MBPP: baseline increases from ~8.7% (idx0) to ~13% (idx1–3). Ablation starts at ~0% (idx0), rises to ~8.4% (idx1), then exceeds baseline at ~16.6% (idx2–3). This is a striking pattern: the ablation underperforms early but overperforms later, implying strong interaction with configuration (e.g., more stages allow the fixed-rate variant to exploit corrections better).
- Overall: semantic error reduction does not monotonically track pass@1 across datasets/configs; you likely need to decompose failures into semantic-vs-algorithmic and show how the component shifts the distribution.
- For the ablation-study narrative, this plot suggests strong dataset-specific sensitivity and possible negative interaction effects: the component helps synthetic consistently, but may hinder later-stage semantic error reduction on MBPP/contests (or the metric definition differs). This warrants checking metric consistency and whether “semantic error reduction” is computed relative to different baselines per config.

Performance Summary Heatmap: Baseline vs Ablation
- The heatmap summarizes best and average Pass@1 for baseline vs ablation, plus gaps.
- Synthetic: baseline best 0.988 vs ablation best 0.963 (gap 0.025). Baseline avg 0.966 vs ablation avg 0.947 (gap 0.019). This is the clearest win for baseline, both peak and mean.
- Code contests: baseline best 0.375 vs ablation best 0.375 (gap 0). Baseline avg 0.375 vs ablation avg 0.367 (gap 0.008). So baseline improves consistency/average but not peak.
- MBPP: baseline best 0.900 vs ablation best 0.900 (gap 0). Baseline avg 0.899 vs ablation avg 0.892 (gap 0.006). Again, small average advantage.
